title: And, friends, what is yearning for the future? 'My eye in the future will be such and such, forms will be such and such, and…
content: And, friends, what is yearning for the future? 'My eye in the future will be such and such, forms will be such and such, and he directs his mind to obtain what is not yet obtained.' Because of that directing of mind, he delights in that. Delighting in that, he yearns for the future. 'My ear in the future will be such and such, sounds will be such and such... My nose in the future will be such and such, odors will be such and such... My tongue in the future will be such and such, flavors will be such and such... My body in the future will be such and such, tangibles will be such and such... My mind in the future will be such and such, mental objects will be such and such, and he directs his mind to obtain what is not yet obtained.' Because of that directing of mind, he delights in that. Delighting in that, he yearns for the future. Thus, friends, is yearning for the future.

title: Having known all abodes, not longing for any, That sage is truly a sage, without greed, without clinging, No longer striving, having reached the far…
content:
Having known all abodes, not longing for any,
That sage is truly a sage, without greed, without clinging,
No longer striving, having reached the far shore.

All-conquering, all-knowing, of perfect wisdom,
Undefiled regarding all things, abandoning all,
With craving destroyed, liberated —
The wise know that one as a sage.

Endowed with the power of wisdom, virtuous, observant,
Calm, delighting in jhāna, mindful,
Freed from all bonds, without taints —
The wise know that one as a sage.

title: Elder Son of Jambugāmika: Are you satisfied with the robe you wear? Are you delighted with adornments? This fragrance emanates from your virtue, Not from…
content:
Elder Son of Jambugāmika:

Are you satisfied with the robe you wear?
Are you delighted with adornments?
This fragrance emanates from your virtue,
Not from others producing such a scent!

title: - From how many things, Venerable Sir, does the Tathāgata dwell withdrawn, detached, liberated, with unlimited mind? - From ten things, Bāhuna, does the Tathāgata…
content:
- From how many things, Venerable Sir, does the Tathāgata dwell withdrawn, detached, liberated, with unlimited mind?
- From ten things, Bāhuna, does the Tathāgata dwell withdrawn, detached, liberated, with unlimited mind. What ten? From form, Bāhuna, the Tathāgata dwells withdrawn, detached, liberated, with unlimited mind. From feeling, Bāhuna... From perception, Bāhuna... From formations, Bāhuna... From consciousness, Bāhuna... From birth, Bāhuna... From aging, Bāhuna... From death, Bāhuna... From this suffering, Bāhuna... From defilements, Bāhuna, the Tathāgata dwells withdrawn, detached, liberated, with unlimited mind. Just as, Bāhuna, a blue lotus, or a red lotus, or a white lotus, born in the water, grown up in the water, rises up above the water and stands without being stained by the water. So too, Bāhuna, from these ten things, the Tathāgata dwells withdrawn, detached, liberated, with unlimited mind.

title: One who knows the passing away and rebirth of beings everywhere, Not attached, well-gone, Awakened, enlightened — Him I call a brahmin. One whose destination…
content:
One who knows the passing away and rebirth of beings everywhere,
Not attached, well-gone,
Awakened, enlightened —
Him I call a brahmin.

One whose destination is not known by devas, gandhabbas, or humans,
A taintless one, an Arahant —
Him I call a brahmin.

One who has nothing in the past, future, or present,
Who has nothing, who clings to nothing —
Him I call a brahmin.

The bull, the excellent, the hero,
The great sage, the conqueror,
The passion-free, the purified, the awakened —
Him I call a brahmin.

One who knows his former lives,
Who sees heaven and hell,
Who has reached the end of birth,
Who has attained direct knowledge, perfected,
The sage, the seer,
Perfect in all accomplishments —
Him I call a brahmin.

title: Bhikkhus, one who does not delight in the earth element does not delight in suffering. One who does not delight in suffering, I say, is…
content: Bhikkhus, one who does not delight in the earth element does not delight in suffering. One who does not delight in suffering, I say, is freed from suffering. Bhikkhus, one who does not delight in the water element ... one who does not delight in the fire element ... one who does not delight in the air element does not delight in suffering. One who does not delight in suffering, I say, is freed from suffering.

title: Bhikkhus, some samaṇas and brahmins misrepresent me falsely, groundlessly, vainly, untruthfully, saying: 'Samaṇa Gotama is a nihilist, he teaches annihilation, destruction, the nonexistence of beings.'…
content: Bhikkhus, some samaṇas and brahmins misrepresent me falsely, groundlessly, vainly, untruthfully, saying: 'Samaṇa Gotama is a nihilist, he teaches annihilation, destruction, the nonexistence of beings.' But I am not like that, I do not say that. Bhikkhus, both formerly and now, I only teach suffering and the cessation of suffering. Bhikkhus, if others revile, abuse, and anger the Tathāgata, then, bhikkhus, the Tathāgata has no hatred, no bitterness, no displeasure. Bhikkhus, if others honor, respect, revere, and make offerings to the Tathāgata, then, bhikkhus, the Tathāgata has no joy, no delight, no pleasure. Bhikkhus, if others honor, respect, revere, and make offerings to the Tathāgata, then, bhikkhus, the Tathāgata thinks: 'This is something that was already known before; here is a task I must do.'

title: Bhikkhus, the multitude is immersed in ignorance, blind, covered and fettered. When the Dhamma that subdues ignorance is taught by the Tathāgata, they listen well,…
content: Bhikkhus, the multitude is immersed in ignorance, blind, covered and fettered. When the Dhamma that subdues ignorance is taught by the Tathāgata, they listen well, lend an ear, and apply their minds with right understanding. This, bhikkhus, is the fourth wonderful, marvelous quality that appears when the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One appears.

title: And, Ānanda, how is a bhikkhu an island unto himself, a refuge unto himself, with no other refuge, having the Dhamma as his island, the…
content: And, Ānanda, how is a bhikkhu an island unto himself, a refuge unto himself, with no other refuge, having the Dhamma as his island, the Dhamma as his refuge, with no other refuge? Ānanda, here a bhikkhu dwells contemplating the body as a body, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and displeasure in regard to the world; he dwells contemplating feelings as feelings ... mind as mind ... phenomena as phenomena, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and displeasure in regard to the world. Ānanda, that is how a bhikkhu is an island unto himself, a refuge unto himself, with no other refuge, having the Dhamma as his island, the Dhamma as his refuge, with no other refuge. Ānanda, those who now or after my passing are islands unto themselves, refuges unto themselves, with no other refuge, having the Dhamma as their island, the Dhamma as their refuge, with no other refuge — these, Ānanda, will be the foremost among my bhikkhus, if they are eager to learn.

title: Elder Nun Sumedhā (continued): What is this foul body, Stinking, causing fear, A bag of dead flesh, Oozing impure fluids? What is this body I…
content:
Elder Nun Sumedhā (continued):

What is this foul body,
Stinking, causing fear,
A bag of dead flesh,
Oozing impure fluids?

What is this body I know,
Disgusting, covered with blood and flesh,
A dwelling for worms,
Food for birds?
This body is thus —
To whom would I give it now?

The body, left in a charnel ground,
Before long, without consciousness,
Like a discarded log,
Despised even by relatives.

That body, thrown in a charnel ground,
Becomes food for others.
Formerly they washed it clean,
Now they are disgusted by it.
Even parents are thus —
What to say of the crowd?

They are infatuated with a body without core,
Bound by sinews and bones,
Full of saliva, full of tears,
Full of foul excrement and urine.

If the body were cut open,
Turning the inside out,
Even the mother would be disgusted,
Unable to bear the stench.

It is through aggregates, sense spheres, and elements
That these bodies are formed,
The root of renewed existence, of suffering.
They rightly say
They have no delight at all.
Therefore, whom would I want to marry?

Every day, a hundred spears
Always stabbing the body anew,
Even if harmed for a hundred years —
That would still be better,
For that suffering would finally end.

The wise person would consent
To such killing as described above.
The Teacher taught that
They wander for a long time,
With a mind that kills,
Continuously, one after another.

In the realms of devas and humans,
Animals, asuras,
Ghosts, and hells,
They suffer countless killings.

In hell, many
With defiled minds, fallen,
Find no refuge in heaven,
No bliss of Nibbāna.

Those who attain Nibbāna
Are intimately connected
With the teachings
Of the One with Ten Powers,
Living at ease, not restless,
Striving to end birth and death.

Dear father, today
I am determined to leave.
Those empty possessions
Have no value at all.
I am disgusted with sensual desires,
I am weary of sensual desires.
They have been cut apart
Like a tāla palm trunk.
